I view this as less of an issue actually.  We can simply restrict names that get decorated in the metaclass.  For example, `test_after_full_execution` gets decorated the way you are doing now.  For tests that want to inspect the exhale configurations (since currently by the time the test method executes exhale is finished), we could actually cheat and enable an exhale-specific internal setting.  E.g. if the base class defines `test_after_apply_sphinx_configurations`, then the decorated `confoverrides` could set something like `exhale_args["TEST STOP AFTER"] = "apply_sphinx_configurations"`.  This would not be very difficult for me to implement, and also serves as a nice debugging feature if people really want it.

AKA for the time being, I'll just decorate `test_after_full_execution` to have it ready for windows, and then in the distance expose better "break points" in the execution lifecycle that can be tested individually.

```
@pytest.mark.django_db
def test_save_deferred_field_with_update_fields():
    TestModel.objects.create()

    tm = TestModel.objects.defer('boolean').first()
    tm.boolean = False
    tm.save(update_fields=['boolean'])
```

We have a name convention for this case:
* If method or attribute is supposed or safe to use as is on instantiated variables or classes outside of class code, we have no initial underscores (`public_method`)
* If it's not safe or some consideration need to be made, or it just internal method (like in this case) and doesn't make sense outside the class, then we use single underscore as a prefix (`_protected_method`)
* If we want to alert not only those who use our class, but also those who extends is, we use double underscore as a prefix (`__private_method`) which enables name mangling
* and of course there is also `__special_methods__` :)

Of course it's no Java encapsulation and we still can access all the methods and attributes, but with this convention we clarify our intent about scope of methods and attributes.

@akx Sorry for my late reply. This is created to support "Enums defined as nested classes now can be migrated". An example for this is

```python
# module: demo.hello.models

from django.db import models
from enumfields import IntEnum, EnumIntegerField

class Order(models.Model):
    class State(IntEnum):
        PENDING, ERROR, DONE = xrange(3)

    state = EnumIntegerField(State, default=State.PENDING)
```


As I found it's sometimes more elegant to define an enum as a nested class of a Django Model class (in that case, it's `Order.State`). So in this case, the `enum` parameter passed to `EnumField` is a `tuple` of 2 strings: `('demo.hello.models', 'Order.State')`, and it will also work with deeper-nested classes...

Reference: https://github.com/hzdg/django-enumfields/pull/68/files#diff-0d0002b8d572eae99755278bc4aeb328R110

My initial thinking for placing it outside of CI is that I didn't want developers (inc 3rd party) to think that it's only for Continuous Integration systems. Developers should be able to discover and use this script to build the manylinux wheel without being mislead that it's for ci only. Also, by having it outside of `ci/`, we're less likely to change the script in such a way that it only works in our ci builds (ci-specific environment variables, etc.).

The idea behind memcpy_s is to ensure you have enough buffer space to copy the data on each call. Instead of passing initiator_length as the second param you want to pass data_length. Then, in the next call, data_length - initiator_length, then in the next call data_length - initiate_length - acceptor_length, etc. The function can only protect you if you tell it how much buffer space is actually left.

See https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/wes2t00f.aspx

> Why? Nothing prevents you from doing this.

Sure... and nothing prevents you from wasting effort by putting an expensive method call inside a loop...

```python
for i in range(0, 100):
  result = expensive_method_that_could_be_outside()
  topic.publish(result + " " + i)
```

If you ran that code, you'd go "wtf, why does this take so long?" and then change `validate` to `False` or -- even better -- move it out of the loop... That's not even Python specific... it's just looping: don't put anything in a loop unless it needs to be there.

That said, considering that unlike boto's `get_bucket()`, no extra information comes back with the topic (ie, the response just sends back "yes here's the name you sent us...") I don't think this is super important.

If topic starts including extra pieces of information (say... a throughput limit of X messages per second?) then it seems like constructing a Topic object on its own means you're missing out on some important information, and `.get_topic` (with `validate=True` by default) seems like a good choice. If/when that debate comes up, we can discuss it (obviously making `.topic` do a lookup down the road would be bad... as everyone who treated it as though it wasn't making an API call will now get shot in the foot).

Side note: looks like it would be valuable to have a parametrized fixture in conftest that returns all mainnet VMs. Then this would reduce to two cases, and we wouldn't have to remember to update the test on every new fork, like:

```py
@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    'base, exponent, expected',
    (
        (0, 1, 0,),
        (0, 0, 1,),
    )
)
def test_exp(mainnet_vm, base, exponent, expected):
    computation = prepare_computation(mainnet_vm)
    computation.stack_push(exponent)
    computation.stack_push(base)
    computation.opcodes[opcode_values.EXP](computation)
    result = computation.stack_pop(type_hint=constants.UINT256)
    assert result == expected
```

I was hoping to merge the manually entered and the generated topic arguments together, but I realize that that would yield filter results that are entirely different than adding the results from the two sets of filtered data.  e.g. merging (A, B) with (C, D) to get a topic list like ((A, C), (B, D)).  The merged topic list would match AB, AD, CB, CD instead of just AC, BD.  For this reason, manually entering topics needs to either be disabled, or throw an exception if topics have already been generated.

`create_title` currently looks like this if you're writing tests for `LazyFormatter`. I had to change it so that it could supply a default value.
```
def create_title(plugin=None):
    if args.title and plugin:
        _title = _author = _category = _game = None
        
        title = LazyFormatter.format(maybe_encode(args.title),
                                     title=[plugin.get_title(),DEFAULT_STREAM_METADATA["title"]],
                                     author=[plugin.get_author(),DEFAULT_STREAM_METADATA["author"]],
                                     category=[plugin.get_category(),DEFAULT_STREAM_METADATA["category"]],
                                     game=[plugin.get_category(),DEFAULT_STREAM_METADATA["game"]])
    else:
        title = args.url
    return title
```
